hinge: Generalized ulceration with low-grade fever (37°C) suggests desquamative gingivitis or viral stomatitis. CHX mouthwash for 2 weeks with re-evaluation is the appropriate initial management, providing antimicrobial coverage while avoiding instrumentation that would traumatize ulcerated tissues.

Gingival overgrowth (pseudopocket/false pocket) with no attachment loss is treated with external bevel gingivectomy. Internal bevel gingivectomy is used in periodontal flap surgery for pocket elimination; crown lengthening is for restorative purposes.
The primary clinical function of a periodontal probe is to assess bleeding on probing (BOP), pocket depth, and attachment levels. Checking caries and excavation are not periodontal probe functions.

For recession with food impaction under the contact area, an interdental brush is the best tool as it can be sized to fit the open embrasure space and mechanically dislodge food debris. Super floss is for under-bridge cleaning; floss is for tight contacts.

Yellowish-colored soft deposits that cannot be easily dislodged with water spray describe dental plaque — a biofilm that adheres tenaciously to tooth surfaces. Pellicle is the thin acellular film; materia alba is loosely adherent and easily removed with spray.

A medically healthy patient with 5 mm probing depth, no bleeding, no gingival inflammation, and 4 mm attached gingiva may represent a pseudopocket from gingival enlargement. However, the absence of bleeding and inflammation raises the possibility of healthy periodontium with deeper probing due to thick tissue — a healthy periodontium variant.

For a smoker with deep pockets requiring topical local-delivery antibiotics, minocycline gel is preferred as it has activity against the broad spectrum of periodontal pathogens and good substantivity in the pocket. Doxycycline gel is also used but minocycline is the more standard board answer for localized delivery.

In cyclosporine-induced gingival enlargement with severe overgrowth, treating the gingival enlargement first (via surgical excision) will address the malalignment (by reducing bulky tissue), improve OH access, and reduce halitosis. The enlargement is the primary driver of the secondary problems.

Poorly controlled diabetes with rising HbA1c (8, 9, 11) is a contraindication for implant placement and bone grafting. Phase I treatment should address acute infection and periodontal disease, and all surgical/implant procedures should be deferred until glycemic control improves.

Recession with deep pocket isolated to the MB cusp/root of maxillary first molar suggests a root-specific problem. Root amputation of the affected MB root with endodontic treatment of remaining roots is appropriate to preserve the tooth. GTR may not be feasible with through-and-through furcation involvement.

During scaling, the instrument shank should be positioned parallel to the long axis of the tooth to allow proper angulation of the working end (cutting edge) against the root surface at approximately 70-80 degrees for effective calculus removal.

Short clinical crowns with excessive gingival display (12 mm sulcus depth indicates altered passive eruption) and good occlusion require crown lengthening to expose more clinical crown and improve smile esthetics. Gingivectomy alone would remove keratinized tissue without addressing the osseous level.

Grade II furcation with generalized horizontal bone loss in a tooth that can be preserved should receive GTR for furcation regeneration. Furcation plasty is for Grade I; root resection is for Grade III or severe root-specific disease.

Before prosthetic crown treatment, bleeding and shallow pockets (2-3 mm) indicate gingivitis. SRP, polishing, and re-evaluation must be completed to control inflammation before crown preparation, as inflamed tissues will not provide an adequate impression or long-term crown margin health.

During exams (stress-induced), foul odor without other systemic symptoms is classic for NUG. The first step in treatment is gentle superficial scaling/debridement to remove necrotic debris and pseudomembrane, followed by CHX rinses and improved OH.

New anterior crowns with erythema, bleeding, inflammation, and bad odor 4 weeks post-placement is classic biologic width violation — the crown margins extend too close to or below the alveolar crest, causing persistent inflammation. Allergy and plasma cell gingivitis are less likely with this acute localized presentation.

Recession with metal exposure 3 months after 6-year-old crowns is most likely caused by traumatic toothbrushing with excessive force, which causes gingival recession over time. BW violation would cause inflammation from the start; excess cement would cause early inflammation, not delayed recession.

Clinical attachment loss (CAL) = probing depth + recession. A 5 mm probing depth with 2 mm gingival recession equals 7 mm of CAL, indicating the total attachment loss from the CEJ to the base of the pocket.

Thin gingival biotype is most commonly associated with fenestration and dehiscence — areas where the alveolar bone is absent, covered only by thin mucosa. McCall festoons and Stillman clefts are gingival changes associated with occlusal trauma.

Recession with reduced interdental papilla requires esthetic periodontal surgery (root coverage procedures ± papilla reconstruction) to address the cosmetic concern. Pocket reduction and osseous surgery address disease, not esthetic recession.

Gingival thickness is assessed clinically by probing through the gingiva (transgingival probing) using a periodontal probe with anesthesia, or non-invasively by visual/clinical assessment. Among listed instruments, the periodontal probe is the most appropriate tool for transgingival probing.

Surgical exposure of an impacted canine often results in gingival recession on the labial surface of the erupted tooth due to thin or absent keratinized tissue, unfavorable eruption path, and manipulation of the thin buccal gingiva during surgery.

Before root amputation or resection, adequate keratinized tissue is critical for long-term maintenance and plaque control around the remaining roots. Insufficient keratinized tissue leads to mucosal inflammation and failure of the hemisection or amputation.

Class II furcation with 4 mm depth and bleeding in an otherwise good OH patient requires flap surgery (open flap debridement ± regeneration) for direct access to the furcation for thorough root planing and regeneration material placement. Furcation plasty is for Grade I only.

A diabetic patient with calculus, severe bleeding, and deep pockets (5-6 mm) requires mechanical cleaning (SRP) combined with chlorhexidine gel for enhanced antimicrobial effect during the initial phase. Systemic antibiotics may be needed later but mechanical debridement + chlorhexidine is the first line.

When two teeth are moved apart orthodontically, the interdental papilla adapts onto the bone and stretches, maintaining its shape rather than splitting. The papilla has remarkable capacity to remodel and follow the teeth as they separate, provided the interproximal bone height is maintained.

Minocycline microspheres (local delivery) are designed to release the antibiotic over approximately 14 days in the periodontal pocket, maintaining therapeutic concentrations for sustained antimicrobial effect.

Gingival crevicular fluid (GCF) flow increases significantly during active inflammation due to increased vascular permeability of the sulcular and junctional epithelium, allowing more serum-derived fluid and inflammatory mediators to enter the sulcus.

The minimum distance between the restoration margin and the alveolar bone crest is 3-4 mm to preserve the biologic width (2 mm supracrestal tissue attachment) and allow for a 1-2 mm sulcus depth without violating the attachment apparatus.

The overhanging restoration on #16 with localized deep pocket (5 mm) adjacent to the overhang in a diabetic patient with generalized bone loss indicates the localized bone resorption is primarily caused by the defective restoration acting as a plaque trap. Diabetes contributes to generalized susceptibility but the overhang is the direct local cause of the mesial defect.
